title: Lens intuition
description: Test your understanding of images formed by lenses.

assessment_items.question_content:
Below is a diagram showing a lens with focal length $f$ focusing an object $o$ units in front of the lens to an image $i$ units behind the lens.

![Thin lens diagram](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/048c90113aa1466e707cbd33c46503bb.png)

If the object moves farther from the lens, what happens to the image?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Since the focal length doesn't change, the parallel ray is refracted by the same amount.

![Diagram showing the parallel ray when the object moves away from the lens](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/4dd12815ddce2f194fc9b7edf79bcb00.png)
The medial ray, which passes through the center of the lens, will get less steep as the object moves farther from the lens.

Since the medial ray is less steep, it intersects with the parallel ray at a point closer to the lens.

![Diagram showing object further from the lens](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/76e210e67cb5c2d3845d6c5cbcea2b7e.png)

So when the object moves away from the lens, the image moves closer to the lens.

choices.content: The image moves closer to the lens.
The image stays where it is.
The image moves away from the lens.

assessment_items.question_content:
Below is a diagram showing a lens with focal length $f$ focusing an object $o$ units in front of the lens to an image $i$ units behind the lens.

![Thin lens diagram](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/048c90113aa1466e707cbd33c46503bb.png)

If the object moves closer to the lens, what happens to the image?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Since the focal length doesn't change, the parallel ray is refracted by the same amount.

![Diagram showing the parallel ray when the object moves closer to the lens](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/4afbe3eae89904f58b31362d2686c05a.png)
The medial ray, which passes through the center of the lens, will get steeper as the object moves closer to the lens.

Since the medial ray is steeper, it intersects with the parallel ray at a point farther from the lens, at a greater distance from the center of the lens.

![Diagram showing object closer to the lens](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c86a30385d03e26e9c5bf9fd6379e1db.png)

So when the object moves towards the lens, the image gets bigger.

choices.content: The image gets smaller.
The image stays the same size.
The image gets bigger.

assessment_items.question_content:
Below is a diagram showing a lens with focal length $f$ focusing an object $o$ units in front of the lens to an image $i$ units behind the lens.

![Thin lens diagram](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/048c90113aa1466e707cbd33c46503bb.png)

What happens to the position of the image if we make the object smaller?

[[☃ radio 2]]

What happens to the size of the image if we make the object smaller?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: All points in a vertical plane through the object are focused to the same focal plane.

So making the object smaller doesn't affect the position of the image, but the image will be smaller.

![Diagram showing the parallel ray when the object moves away fromt the lens](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/b07f8913ca58dbabf77f1a9b44de056e.png)

choices.content: The image gets smaller.
The image stays the same size.
The image gets bigger.
The image moves closer to the lens.
The image stays where it is.
The image moves away from the lens.

assessment_items.question_content:
Below is a diagram showing a lens with focal length $f$ focusing an object $o$ units in front of the lens to an image $i$ units behind the lens.

![Thin lens diagram](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/86ae18426a25c726c56a7f4da4644d07.png)

What happens to the image when we decrease the focal length?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Since the object has not changed position, the medial ray does not change position.

![Diagram showing the medial ray when the focal length decreases](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/fe56d932c03733463ffc33cb631712d4.png)
The parallel ray is refracted so it goes through the focal point. So when the focal point is moved closer to the lens, the refracted ray becomes steeper.

Since the parallel ray is steeper, it intersects with the medial ray at a point closer to the lens.

![Diagram showing a smaller focal length](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/54f004cc4a2879948faa88924c3a7d70.png)

So when we decrease the focal length, the image moves closer to the lens.

choices.content: The image moves closer to the lens.
The image stays where it is.
The image moves away from the lens.

assessment_items.question_content:
Below is a diagram showing a lens with focal length $f$ focusing an object $o$ units in front of the lens to an image $i$ units behind the lens.

![Thin lens diagram](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/aba5ea0b66b7a602b2fddf58240aeda6.png)

If the object moves away from the lens, how can we ensure that the distance between the image and the lens, *i*, stays the same?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
As the object moves away from the lens, the image moves closer to the lens.

![Diagram showing the object farther from the lens](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/df721bef665d8434a4c5bc859e53e114.png)

In order to move the image back to its original position, we need to increase the focal length.

![Diagram with focal length increased](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/961a8c745c5ffb1a53094e6d98be2d24.png)

choices.content: Increase the focal length.
Decrease the focal length.
It's not possible to keep the image at the same distance.

assessment_items.question_content:
Below is a diagram showing a lens with focal length of $4$ units, focusing an object $8$ units in front of the lens. The image is focused $8$ units behind the lens and is the same size as the object.

![Thin lens diagram](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/414c77864c1af9cb57416e70ec1d0b76.png)

What happens if the object moves closer to the lens so that $o = f$?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: If the height of the object is $h$, then the slope of the medial ray is $\dfrac{h}{o}$. The slope of the parallel ray once it is refracted is $\dfrac{h}{f}$.

If $o = f$, then the slope of the two rays is equal.

![Diagram showing the object further from the lens](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/fed29f1c49d3eabc978f9351aeed274e.png)

Since the two rays have the same slope, they will never intersect. If there is no intersection point, then there is no point at which the image is in focus.

It will be impossible to focus the image.

choices.content: The image is impossible to focus.
The image becomes infinitely small.
The image is no longer inverted.
